name: Lumbar Disc Herniation
anatomy_overview: The lumbar spine consists of five vertebrae (L1–L5) stacked between the thoracic spine and the sacrum. Between each vertebra sits an intervertebral disc — a cushion of tough outer cartilage (annulus fibrosus) surrounding a gel-like core (nucleus pulposus). These discs absorb shock and allow flexible movement.
explanation: In this simulated scan, the highlighted area suggests a possible disc bulge near the L4–L5 junction. When the soft inner core of a disc pushes through a tear in the outer layer, it may press on nearby nerve roots. This is a common finding in adults aged 30–50 and does not always cause symptoms.
what_it_may_represent: Mild disc bulge without nerve compression — often asymptomatic
Disc herniation pressing on a nearby nerve root, which may cause radiating leg pain (sciatica)
Degenerative disc changes consistent with normal ageing
Note: Only a qualified radiologist can interpret your actual scan.
doctor_questions: What exactly does this finding mean for my specific situation?
Is this level of disc change typical for my age?
Could this explain the pain or symptoms I have been experiencing?
What non-surgical options are available first — such as physical therapy or pain management?
Are there activities I should avoid to prevent further irritation?
When would surgery become a consideration, and what would that involve?
Do I need any follow-up imaging, such as a repeat MRI or nerve conduction study?
Should I see a specialist — such as a neurologist, orthopaedic surgeon, or pain specialist?

additional_imaging: Follow-up MRI in 6–12 weeks if symptoms change
CT scan if bone detail is needed alongside soft tissue findings
Nerve conduction study (EMG) if nerve involvement is suspected
conservative_treatments: Guided physical therapy to strengthen core and back muscles
Over-the-counter anti-inflammatory medication (as directed by a doctor)
Heat or ice therapy for short-term relief
Activity modification — avoiding heavy lifting or prolonged sitting
specialist_referrals: Primary care physician — first point of contact to review the scan
Orthopaedic surgeon or neurosurgeon — if structural intervention is considered
Pain management specialist — for ongoing pain that does not respond to first-line care
Physiotherapist — for a personalised rehabilitation programme

urgent_care_signs: Sudden loss of bladder or bowel control
Rapidly worsening weakness in one or both legs
Severe, unrelenting pain that does not improve with rest
Numbness in the groin or inner thigh area (saddle anaesthesia)
